facts.description: Every Utah resident driving on public highways must have a valid Utah driver license, learner permit, or temporary driver license
Becomes a Utah resident if in state 6+ months/year, registers vehicle here, obtains Utah DL, or gainfully employed (not temporary)
Visitors with valid out-of-state/country license may drive in Utah up to 6 months
Out-of-state LEARNER PERMITS are NOT valid in Utah
Minimum age for a Utah Class D driver license is 16
Under 18 must hold learner permit 6 months AND complete approved driver education
Age 19+ with no prior license may be licensed without driver ed if permit held 90 days + 40 hours practice driving
Cannot have a Utah DL if driving privilege is denied/suspended/revoked/disqualified in Utah OR any other state
Utah is a member of the Driver License Compact (DLC) and Non-Resident Violator Compact (NRVC) - out-of-state convictions forwarded to home state
Class D = regular driver license for personal vehicles up to 26,000 lbs
Class C = single vehicle under 26,001 lbs carrying 16+ occupants or placarded hazmat; min age 21
Class B = single/combined vehicle over 26,001 lbs with towed unit over 10,001 lbs
Class A = combined vehicle 26,001+ lbs with towed unit over 10,001 lbs
Commercial Driver License required for vehicles with GVWR 26,001+ lbs, or designed for 16+ passengers, hazmat, doubles/triples, tankers, school buses
Anyone under 21 with Class D gets a Provisional license - uses separate point system
Limited-Term DL issued to non-U.S. Citizens/Nationals/LPRs with lawful presence proof
Driving Privilege Card (DPC) is for driving only - NOT valid ID or age verification; expires every birthday
An interdicted DL/IDC has a red 'NO ALCOHOL SALE' banner at the top
M = Motorcycle endorsement required to ride a motorcycle on Utah highways
H = Hazmat endorsement
T = Doubles/Triples endorsement
X = Hazmat + Tanker combined endorsement
S = School Bus endorsement
Z = Taxicab endorsement
N=Tanker, P=Passenger - other endorsement letters
Restriction B = corrective lenses required
Restriction A = no restrictions
Restriction G = daylight hours only
Written knowledge test passes at 80%; untimed; generally 30-45 minutes
Two written tests per day allowed; three attempts per fee within 12 months
Traffic Safety & Trends Exam: ONLINE only, must score 100%, required for all first-time Class D applicants
Skills test minimum age at the DLD is 16
Skills test minimum age is 15 with a Third-Party Tester or high school program
You must provide your own vehicle for the skills test
Only the applicant and examiner are allowed in the vehicle during the skills test
Skills tests are by appointment only
Third-Party Testers administer the skills test ONLY - not the written knowledge test
Automatic skills-test failures: moving violation, fail to yield to pedestrians, avoidable crash, forcing evasive action, sidewalk/curb violations, examiner takes control, too fast/slow
Utah vision standard: 20/40 vision AND 90 degree peripheral field in at least one eye
If you cannot meet vision standard even with glasses, you need a written eye-doctor statement
Vision test required on field-office applications; ages 65+ tested with EVERY license application
Driver has final responsibility for knowing whether they are safe to drive - not doctor or DLD
Skipping a required Review Examination = driving privilege withdrawn
Documents must be original or certified copies - faxed/photocopied not accepted
2 documents required as proof of Utah residency, each dated within 90 days
Must notify DLD of address change within 10 days of moving
Provisional (under 21) license expires 5 years from issue; Regular (21+) expires 8 years - both on birthday
May renew license up to 6 months before expiration
A duplicate (replacement) license keeps the same expiration date as the original
Utah does NOT allow holding a Utah DL and a Utah ID card simultaneously
Entry-level learner permit minimum age is 15
Entry-level learner permit validity is 18 months
Permit holder must have parent/guardian/instructor OR licensed 21+ adult (with authorization) in the front seat
Permit must be in immediate possession while driving
Entry-level learner permit fee is $19.00
6-month/renewal learner permit minimum age is 16
Learner permit hold time is 6 months for ages 16-17
Learner permit hold time is 90 days for age 19+ (waivable with driver ed)
Driver education requires 40 hours of practice driving including 10 hours after sunset with parent/guardian
Driver ed is required (mandatory) for ages 15-18
Age 16 licensed drivers cannot drive 12:00 a.m. - 5:00 a.m.
Age 16 curfew exceptions: 21+ in front seat, agricultural assignment, to/from work, to/from school activity, emergency
Age 16 licensed drivers may carry immediate family only until 6 months from license issue
Age 17 has no curfew or passenger restrictions (license holders)
Regular Class D license minimum age is 21
Leave 10 inches between chest and steering wheel
Top of steering wheel should be no higher than your shoulders, below chin level
Headrest contacts the back of your head above the level of your ears
Adjust left mirror with head against closed left window so you barely see rear left edge of vehicle
Adjust right mirror by leaning right, so you barely see the right side of vehicle
Everyone in the vehicle must have a fastened safety belt or child seat
Children 8 and younger must be in a child/booster seat unless they are 57 inches tall
Rear-facing in back seat until at least 2 years and 30 lbs (or longer if seat allows)
Forward-facing with harness in back seat until at least 4 years and 40 lbs
Booster seat until belt fits and child is 57 inches tall
Children 12 and younger should ride in the back seat
Shoulder belt across shoulder/chest; lap belt across hips, NOT stomach; shoulder belt NOT under arm or behind back
Officer may stop you solely if anyone under 16 is unrestrained (primary offense)
Smoking in a vehicle while transporting children 15 and younger is an infraction
Air bags work WITH safety belts - not as a replacement
Air bags deploy at up to 200 mph - sit as far back from air bag as practical
Infants must NEVER ride in the front seat with a passenger-side air bag
Disabling/modifying/removing the air bag system is a Class B misdemeanor
When backing, look through the rear window (mirrors do not show directly behind)
When backing, steer in the direction you want the REAR of vehicle to move
Do NOT back up on a freeway or interstate
Lane change procedure: mirrors, signal at least 2 seconds, head check (blind spot), merge when safe
Do NOT change lanes inside an intersection
Finish a left turn in the left-most lane (next to centerline); finish a right turn in the right-most (curb) lane
Stay in the right-most lane unless it is blocked
Zipper method: at a congested merge, use BOTH lanes until the merge point, then alternate yielding into the single lane
Gore area (triangular area between solid white lines at lane entry/exit) - illegal to cross or drive in
When stopping behind a vehicle, leave enough gap to see the rear tires of the car in front
No parking within 15 feet of a fire hydrant
No parking on a crosswalk or within 20 feet of a crosswalk
No parking within 20 feet of a fire station driveway; if signs posted, 75 feet on opposite side
No parking within 30 feet of a flashing beacon, stop sign, yield sign, or traffic signal
No parking on a railroad track or within 50 feet of the nearest rail
Parked vehicle back wheel must be no more than 12 inches from curb
No parking: interstate shoulder (except breakdown), sidewalk, intersection, bridge/tunnel, in front of driveway, red zones
Downhill parking with curb: turn wheels TOWARD the curb
Uphill parking with curb: turn wheels AWAY from the curb
Uphill parking with no curb: turn wheels to the RIGHT
Before leaving parked vehicle: set parking brake, stop motor, lock doors
On multi-lane roads, the left lane is for passing slower vehicles
Never pass on the shoulder
When returning to right lane after passing, return no less than 200 feet from oncoming vehicles
Never pass when oncoming vehicle present, view blocked by curve/hill, at intersections/crosswalks, before RR crossing/bridge, or in no-passing zone
When passing on the right, illegal to leave the roadway
Do not pass a flashing-yellow-light snowplow on the side where the plow blade is deployed
Do not pass three or more snowplows in echelon on either side
Signal at least 2 seconds before a turn
Signal at least 2 seconds before a lane change
Both hands on the outside of the steering wheel; hand-over-hand or push-pull steering; never hold the inside
Complete stop required at: steady red, flashing red, stop signs, any crash, when police request, entering street from alley/driveway/private road
At controlled railroad crossings, stop more than 15 feet but not more than 50 feet from nearest rail
Right turn: signal 2 sec, head check right shoulder, traffic check during turn, do not cut corner or make wide turn
Left turn: signal 2 sec, head check left shoulder, traffic check during turn, do not cut corner or make wide turn
On multi-lane turns, enter the lane from which you will turn and STAY in that lane until completed
May not drive over, across, or within any part of an island
Never U-turn on a curve or near top of hill where you cannot see 500 feet in both directions
Never U-turn where prohibited by sign, on a railroad track or grade crossing, or on a freeway
Maintain at least 2 seconds following distance on the freeway; increase in adverse conditions
No U-turns on a freeway
Emergency lane outside solid white line is for emergencies only - no driving, passing, or pickup
Never legally drive faster than the posted limit, even matching traffic flow
Vehicle in left lane must move safely right when approached from behind (impeding traffic = citation)
Flex lane: green arrow = open, red X = closed; double-dashed yellow on either side
Yellow pavement lines separate traffic moving in OPPOSING directions
White pavement lines separate traffic moving in the SAME direction
Broken (dashed) line on your side of yellow center line = passing PERMITTED
Solid yellow on your side = passing PROHIBITED in your direction
Double solid yellow lines = passing prohibited in both directions
Double solid white lines (express lane separator) = crossing prohibited
Green light: go through with caution IF the way is clear
Green arrow: proceed in the arrow direction after yielding to vehicles and pedestrians clearing the intersection
Yellow light: the light is about to change to red; stop if you can safely
Flashing yellow: slow down and proceed with caution; be prepared to stop
Flashing yellow arrow: turns are permitted BUT yield to oncoming traffic and pedestrians
Red light: complete stop before the intersection; wait behind stop line/crosswalk until green
Right turn on red allowed after a complete stop, unless posted otherwise
Flashing red: stop and look both ways (treat like a stop sign)
Red arrow: turn prohibited; stop and remain stopped until indication to proceed
If stranded by an unchanging red left arrow, may make left from one-way street onto one-way street if safe
Trains have right-of-way 100% of the time - over ambulances, fire engines, cars, police, and pedestrians
Emergency vehicle with sirens/lights: yield, drive immediately to the RIGHT, STOP until it passes
Police officer directing traffic overrides traffic signals
A flagger in a work zone overrides traffic signs and signals - you must obey
Alternating flashing red lights from a school bus: stop immediately before reaching the bus
Yield to ALL pedestrians at any crosswalk, marked or unmarked
A pedestrian with a white cane or guide dog requires a full STOP
Yield to drivers who arrived at the intersection before you
When turning left, yield to drivers in the opposite (oncoming) lane
At a 4-way stop with simultaneous arrivals, yield to the vehicle on your RIGHT
When traffic lights are dark/non-functioning, come to a complete stop, then yield to any vehicle on your right
Yield to oncoming traffic and pedestrians before entering a roundabout
Roundabouts in the U.S. flow counterclockwise
Signal your intention to exit a roundabout
Slow down on approach to a roundabout
On an entrance ramp, yield to drivers already on the interstate
When entering a public road from a driveway or private road, yield to street traffic
On a narrow/single-lane bridge, the first driver to arrive has the right-of-way
On a narrow mountain road, the downhill driver yields to the uphill driver
Never think you have the right-of-way - right-of-way is given, not taken
Yield to pedestrians, bicyclists, and others still in the intersection
Even with a green light, you must yield to pedestrians in the crosswalk
Never pass a vehicle stopped at a crosswalk - hidden pedestrians may be crossing
School zone with FLASHING lights = obey the slower school-zone speed limit
Pedestrians walking without a sidewalk should walk on the LEFT shoulder facing traffic
Stop BEFORE the crosswalk so other drivers can see the pedestrian
On a 2-lane road, school bus red lights require BOTH directions to stop
On a 4-lane road WITHOUT a median, school bus red lights require BOTH directions to stop
On a divided highway with median (4+ lanes), only same-direction traffic must stop for a school bus
On a 5+ lane road with shared center turn lane, only same-direction traffic must stop for a school bus
Illegal pass of a stopped school bus carries up to a $3,000 fine plus insurance increase
Basic speed law: never drive faster than is reasonably safe for conditions
20 mph in school zones (passing school during recess, opening/closing hours, or flashing lights)
25 mph in any business or residential area
55 mph on major highways, as posted
65/70/75/80 mph on rural interstate highways, with posted transition zones
Flashing lights or bells at a railroad crossing = MUST STOP
Crossbuck (white X-shape RAILROAD CROSSING) = yield to trains
Round yellow sign with X and RR = railroad-crossing advance warning; slow, look, listen
Never drive around or under a lowered railroad gate
Do not stop on railroad tracks; if you must stop for traffic, wait at stop line BEFORE the tracks
A 100-car freight train at 55 mph needs more than a mile (about 18 football fields) to stop
TRAX light rail can reach 65 mph in 21 seconds; never try to beat one
If RR lights appear broken: after full stop, proceed only with 1-mile sight line in both directions and no train
Never pass another vehicle in your direction at a railroad crossing
If your car stalls on RR tracks: everyone OUT, notify authorities; if train comes, run TOWARD the train at an angle AWAY from it
Do not follow within 500 feet of a fire vehicle responding to an alarm
May not drive or park on the same block as a fire vehicle answering an alarm
Move Over Law: approaching ANY stationary vehicle with flashing hazard lights, slow down AND change lanes to a non-adjacent lane if practical
HOV variant: stationary amber lights = change lanes OUT of the HOV lane if practical
Move Over violation: 4-hour live classroom defensive driving course in 90 days; failure = 90-day license suspension
Alcohol and impairing drugs reduce: judgment, vision, color distinction, reaction time
Vision is impacted at 0.02 BAC for all drivers - below the legal limit
Vision is the most important sense used in driving
Impairment starts with the first drink
Impairing drugs include all 3 categories: illegal, over-the-counter, AND prescription
Driving while emotional (worried, excited, afraid, angry, depressed) increases risk
Many medications cause drowsiness or other impairment - ask your physician
Under 21: illegal to purchase, possess, OR drink alcohol
Under-21 BAC limit is 0.00 (zero tolerance) - no alcohol in circulatory system
Under-21 possession/consumption/underage-in-bar: 1-year first-offense license suspension, 2-year second/subsequent
Alcohol suspension ordered before age 16: starts on conviction, extends 1-2 years from 16th birthday
Not-a-Drop Act: under 21 + any measurable alcohol = driving privileges denied for 6 months (first offense)
Not-a-Drop second/subsequent within 10 years: denied 2 years OR until age 21, whichever is longer
Not-a-Drop applies even if you have never been issued a license (denies a future license)
Reinstatement after Not-a-Drop suspension: substance-abuse assessment OR Prime For Life DUI class
Utah adult (21+) BAC limit is 0.05 - the LOWEST in the U.S.
CDL BAC limit is 0.04
Driving while impaired is illegal even if under the legal BAC limit (impaired-to-the-degree law)
Adult first DUI conviction: license suspended 120 days
Adult second or subsequent DUI: 2-year suspension
Officer can confiscate Utah DL on DUI arrest; suspension begins on day 45 after arrest
DUI plea held in abeyance: not on regular MVR but counts as prior for future DUIs; disqualifies CDL
Implied Consent Law: by driving in Utah you have already agreed to chemical testing (breath/blood/urine/oral fluids) if requested by officer
The officer chooses which chemical test to administer
Chemical test refusal (21+): license revoked 18 months first offense, 36 months second/subsequent
Chemical test refusal (under 21): revoked 2 years first; second = 36 months OR until 21, whichever is longer
Driving with any measurable alcohol during alcohol-restricted period = 1-year revocation
BUI = DUI: same laws apply to operating a motorboat under the influence
Alcohol-restricted driver status duration: 2, 3, 5, or 10 years depending on offense
Lifetime alcohol-restricted status triggered by automobile homicide or felony DUI conviction
Ignition Interlock Device = breathalyzer wired to ignition; car will not start if alcohol detected
First DUI (21+) = 18-month Ignition Interlock restriction
First DUI under 21 = 3-year Ignition Interlock restriction
Second/subsequent DUI or alcohol-related reckless or chemical refusal = 2-year IID
Felony DUI = 3-year IID
Automobile homicide = 10-year IID
IID monitoring is at least every 60 days; failure or alcohol attempt extends restriction 60 days each occurrence
Driving while interlock-restricted without IID = impound + 1-year revoke + 2 more years IID
On DUI arrest, officer MAY impound vehicle or motorboat
Owner pays impound: towing + storage + administrative fees
Mandatory revocation if convicted of controlled substance/metabolite in body while driving (including prescriptions if measurable)
Distraction = anything that takes your mind OR body away from driving
Pre-drive distraction prevention: turn off devices, set GPS, eat/groom before leaving, secure loose items
If something unexpected happens while driving (dropped item, bee, contact lens) - pull over immediately
While operating a vehicle on a highway, you may NOT manually write/send/read texts, dial, access internet, view/record video, take photos, or enter data on a wireless device
Drivers over 18 may talk on a phone while driving only with voice commands (hands-free)
Drivers under 18 may use a handheld wireless device only for: medical emergency, reporting safety hazard, reporting criminal activity, or communicating with parent/legal guardian
Texting while driving violation = Class C misdemeanor, max fine $100
Texting violation = Class B misdemeanor if you cause bodily injury or have a prior texting offense
Texting violation may also result in license suspension if a judge orders it
Officer can pull you over solely for observing texting (primary offense)
Aggressive driving = committing moving traffic offenses that endanger persons/property, or assault with a motor vehicle
Road rage = criminal offense by a driver, in response to a roadway incident, with intent to endanger or intimidate another driver
Road rage first conviction: court may suspend license for 1 year
Most people need 7 to 9 hours of sleep
On long trips, plan to stop every 2 hours
A useful power nap is 20 minutes
Drowsiness warnings: forgot last few miles, hit rumble strip, drift in lane, wandering thoughts, yawning, heavy eyes, tailgating, missed signs, near misses
Check medicine labels for drowsiness warnings
6 inches of water reaches most passenger cars' bottoms - causes loss of control or stalling
2 feet of moving water can carry away most vehicles, including SUVs and pickups
Turn around - never cross a flooded roadway
Headlights ON sunset to sunrise OR whenever you cannot clearly see 1,000 ft ahead
Dim high beams within 500 feet of an oncoming vehicle
Dim high beams within 300 feet when following another vehicle
Do not wear sunglasses or colored lenses at night
Increase following distance at night by at least 2 seconds
Hydroplaning starts at speeds over 35 mph
Animal in road: DO NOT swerve - keep vehicle under control in lane

Vulnerable users include pedestrians, riders on animals, farm tractor without enclosed shell, skateboards, skates, bicycles, e-bikes, mopeds, motor-driven cycles, motorized scooters, motorcycles, wheelchairs
Distracting a vulnerable user with intent to cause violence/injury or force off road = traffic citation + points
Bicycles are vehicles on roadways - same rights, privileges, and responsibilities as motorists
Bike lanes are marked with solid white lines
Do not stop, park, or drive in a bike lane (except entering/leaving driveway, officer-directed duty, emergency)
Utah requires a minimum of 3 feet of distance when passing a bicyclist or vulnerable user
No-pass zone for cyclists/mopeds: obstructed-view curve/crest, within 100 feet of intersection or RR crossing or obstructed bridge/viaduct/tunnel
Dim headlights within 500 feet when approaching/overtaking a cyclist at night
Check for cyclists BEFORE opening your door when parked at a curb (Dutch reach)
Bicyclists must NEVER ride impaired
Bicyclist must keep at least one hand on the handlebars
Every bicycle must have a brake that can stop within 25 feet from 10 mph
Bicycle lights required from half-hour before sunrise to half-hour after sunset: white headlight, red taillight/reflector, side reflectors visible at 500 ft
Bicyclists may ride two abreast maximum, only if not impeding traffic
CPSC-approved bicycle helmet is recommended but not required by Utah law
E-bike Class 1: motor assists only while pedaling, cuts off at 20 mph
E-bike Class 2: motor can propel without pedaling, cuts off at 20 mph
E-bike Class 3: pedal-assist only, cuts off at 28 mph, requires a speedometer
Motorcycle riders and passengers under 21 must wear a DOT-approved helmet
All street motorcycles must carry liability insurance
Give motorcycles a full lane - do not share the lane
When following a motorcycle, stay at least 2 seconds back (increase in wet conditions)
Motorcycle turn signals often do NOT self-cancel - don't assume flashing means turning
Lane filtering legal when: 2+ same-direction lanes, posted speed limit <= 45 mph or off-ramp, motorcycle <= 15 mph, vehicle being passed is STOPPED, safe
Lane filtering is NOT allowed on freeway on-ramps
Dead-red rule: motorcycle/moped/bicycle 16+ may proceed after waiting at least 90 seconds if signal hasn't detected them and no other traffic/peds
Trucks are NOT large cars - longer stopping/accelerating distances, wider turns, more weight
On multi-lane highways, tractor-trailers stay in the center lane to help on/off-ramp traffic flow
Average passenger car at 55 mph stops in 130-140 feet (less than half a football field)
A fully loaded tractor-trailer at 55 mph may take more than 400 feet to stop
A loaded truck at 55 mph takes at least 335 feet to stop - 1.5x a car (finals-only number)
If you cannot see the truck driver in their side mirror, the driver cannot see you
Always pass a tractor-trailer completely and on the LEFT side - never linger alongside
Trucks swing wide LEFT before a right turn - do not pinch them on the right
Stop lines at intersections give larger vehicles room - always stop behind the stop line
Tractor-trailers often appear slower than they are - don't underestimate approach speed
Truck blind spots: up to 20 ft in front of the cab, both sides (especially alongside cab), and up to 200 ft behind
The largest truck blind spot is on the RIGHT side - starts behind the cab, runs the length of the truck
When passing a truck, don't pull back in until you see the WHOLE FRONT of the truck in your rearview mirror
When stopped behind a large vehicle on a hill, leave extra room - truck may stall or roll backward
Work zone signs are diamond or rectangular, ORANGE with black letters/symbols
Work zones identified by: orange signs, orange devices, flashing lights, highly visible worker clothing
Speeding in a work zone: court must fine AT LEAST TWICE the regular speeding fine
Slow down in work zones even if no workers are present
Over 80% of work-zone casualties are drivers/passengers/pedestrians, NOT workers
Slow-moving vehicle emblem: orange-and-red triangle on rear = vehicle travels under 25 mph
Farm equipment driver cannot hear your vehicle - pass with care
Golf carts on public roads: only where local ordinance allows; follow bicycle rules; no alcohol while operating
Horse-drawn vehicles and horseback riders have same rights to the road - don't honk or rev
A Class D or CDL license covers a street-legal ATV/UTV/FSV - no special endorsement
Pulled over: move to the RIGHT side (even from HOV), put in PARK, turn off engine, STAY in vehicle, window down, hands on wheel
Keep seat belt fastened during traffic stop; ask passengers to do the same
For documents at a traffic stop, keep one hand on the wheel and reach slowly
An 8-sided (octagonal) red sign with white letters means STOP
A downward-pointing triangle (white with red border) means YIELD
Yellow diamond-shaped signs are WARNING signs
A fluorescent yellow-green pentagon sign indicates a school zone or school crossing
A yellow horizontal pennant (sideways triangle) sign means NO PASSING ZONE
A white X-shape sign marks a RAILROAD CROSSING (crossbuck)
Vertical white rectangles with black text are REGULATORY signs (speed limit, do not pass, etc.)
Horizontal rectangles (green or blue) typically give GUIDE or SERVICE information
Red signs mean STOP, YIELD, or PROHIBITION
Yellow signs are general WARNING signs
Orange signs indicate construction or work zones
Green signs give guide/directional info or indicate permitted movements
Blue signs indicate motorist services (hospitals, rest areas, phones)
Brown signs mark recreation or cultural sites
Fluorescent yellow-green signs warn of pedestrian/school/bicycle activity
STOP sign: come to a complete stop and yield to traffic and pedestrians
YIELD sign: slow or stop if necessary and yield to other traffic and pedestrians
Speed Limit sign: the maximum legal speed for that road
A circle-and-slash over a U-shaped arrow means NO U-TURN
A circle-and-slash over a left-turn arrow means NO LEFT TURN
A circle-and-slash over a right-turn arrow means NO RIGHT TURN
DO NOT ENTER sign: red square with white bar - do not enter the roadway
WRONG WAY sign: you are headed against traffic flow - immediately stop and turn around
ONE WAY sign: traffic only flows in the direction of the arrow
NO TURN ON RED sign: right turn on red is prohibited at this intersection
Yellow diamond with curved arrow = curve in the road ahead; reduce speed
Yellow diamond with sharp right-angle arrow = sharp turn ahead
Yellow diamond with arrows converging = merging traffic ahead
Yellow diamond with lane-ends pattern = lane ends ahead; merge
Yellow diamond showing road split = divided highway begins (or ends)
Yellow diamond with octagon symbol = STOP sign ahead
Yellow diamond with traffic light symbol = signal ahead
Yellow (or yellow-green pentagon) sign with walking-person silhouette = pedestrian crossing
Yellow diamond with bicycle symbol = bicycle crossing
Yellow diamond with leaping-deer silhouette = deer crossing area
Yellow diamond with skidding-car symbol = slippery when wet
Yellow diamond with descending-truck symbol = steep hill ahead
Yellow square plaque with mph numerals = advisory (suggested) speed for a curve or condition
Round yellow sign with X and RR = railroad crossing advance warning
Yellow-green pentagon with two children silhouettes = school crossing/school zone
Orange diamond with text or worker symbol = work zone warning
Orange diamond with flagger silhouette = flagger ahead - obey their directions
Blue square with white H = hospital
Blue square with picnic-table symbol = rest area
Red-white-blue shield = interstate route marker
White heraldic shield = U.S. route marker
Yellow pennant sign (horizontal triangle) = NO PASSING ZONE - begins here
Keep Right sign: stay to the right of the obstruction/median
Red circle-and-slash over P = NO PARKING any time
Orange DETOUR sign = follow alternate route around work zone
For teen drivers 15-19, the leading crash cause is failing to keep in the proper lane
For drivers 65+, the leading crash cause is failing to yield right-of-way
Suspension is temporary loss of driving privilege; revocation requires applying for a new license after a set period
Denial = not allowed to obtain a license; Disqualification = loss of CDL privilege
Cancellation = license cancelled due to a procedural/paperwork error (not punitive)
Careless driving = committing 2 or more moving violations during a single drive, OR 1 violation while distracted by in-vehicle activity
Reckless driving = willful or wanton disregard for safety of persons or property
After a crash: stop, render aid, exchange info, report when required
Utah requires liability insurance on all motor vehicles
A traffic accident report is required when injury, death, or property damage of $1,500 or more occurs
Total stopping distance = perception distance + reaction distance + braking distance
Use headlights whenever windshield wipers are in continuous use
A trailer over 750 lbs gross weight is recommended to have brakes; over 2,000 lbs requires brakes in Utah
Never park on a railroad track
Express lane double-white-line crossing is allowed when followed by an emergency/police vehicle directing you to pull over

HAWK (High-intensity Activated crossWalK) beacon is a pedestrian signal — flashing yellow then solid yellow then solid red then alternating flashing red; treat alternating flashing red like a stop sign
Passengers can be a major source of distraction — especially loud or active ones; new drivers should set expectations before driving
Before driving: phone away, music set, GPS set, mirrors aimed, seat adjusted — handle distractions BEFORE wheels move
